content.text: Root cause found: the host-mounted repo overlays `/clawvis`, including a host `.venv` owned by `nobody:nogroup`, so `uv run` inside `kanban-api` cannot replace `kanban-api` entrypoints and exits with permission error. I‚Äôm applying a minimal compose fix so `kanban-api` uses its own writable venv inside the container.

content.name: ApplyPatch
content.input:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: /home/lgiron/lab_perso/clawvis/docker-compose.yml
@@
     volumes:
       # Full repo RW so setup API can sync skills/ and .claude/ (host checkout replaces /clawvis).
       - .:/clawvis:rw
+      # Keep a container-local virtualenv writable by the runtime user.
+      - /clawvis/.venv
       - ./logs:/logs:rw
*** End Patch

content.input:
*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: /mnt/c/Users/lgiron/Documents/developer-brain/inbox/daily/implementation/clawvis/2026-04-28-kanban-api-startup-fix.md
+## Goal
+- Fix `kanban-api` crash during `make up` where the container exited before reaching healthy state.
+
+## What changed
+- Updated `docker-compose.yml` in service `kanban-api` volumes:
+  - Added anonymous volume mount `/clawvis/.venv`.
+- Decision:
+  - Keep a container-local writable virtualenv so `uv run` does not mutate host `.venv` files mounted from the repository.
+
+## Commands and verification
+- `docker logs clawvis-lgiron-kanban-api`
+  - Error observed: `failed to remove file /clawvis/.venv/.../bin/kanban-api: Permission denied`
+- `ls -ld .venv .venv/bin .venv/bin/kanban-api`
+  - Host `.venv` owned by `nobody:nogroup`, not writable for runtime UID.
+- `docker compose up -d kanban-api`
+- `docker compose up -d`
+- `docker compose ps`
+  - `kanban-api` now `Up ... (healthy)`.
+  - Full stack now running and healthy.
+
+## Follow-ups
+- Optional hardening: avoid bind-mounting full repo in production paths where host `.venv` ownership can drift.
*** End Patch

content.name: ApplyPatch
content.input:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: /home/lgiron/lab_perso/clawvis/CHANGELOG.md
@@
 All notable changes to this project will be documented in this file.
 
+## [Unreleased]
+
+### Docker ‚Äî kanban-api startup permission fix (2026-04-28)
+- **Root cause**: `kanban-api` bind-mounted the full repo at `/clawvis`, reusing host `.venv` files owned by `nobody:nogroup`; `uv run` failed with `Permission denied` while replacing `bin/kanban-api`, so the container exited on boot.
+- **Fix**: `docker-compose.yml` now mounts an anonymous volume at `/clawvis/.venv` for `kanban-api`, keeping a container-local writable virtualenv while preserving the host repo bind mount.
+- **Result**: `kanban-api` reaches healthy state and full `docker compose up -d` stack starts successfully.
+
 ## [v1.0.0] - 2026-04-17
*** End Patch

input.description: Show modified files after fix
Run brain sync at session end

content.text:
`kanban-api` crash is fixed and the stack is running.

What was happening:
- `kanban-api` started with repo bind-mounted at `/clawvis`.
- That included host `.venv` files owned by `nobody:nogroup`.
- On startup, `uv run` tried to update `/clawvis/.venv/bin/kanban-api` and failed with permission denied, so container exited (`code 2`).

What I changed:
- Updated `docker-compose.yml` for `kanban-api` volumes:
  - kept `.:/clawvis:rw`
  - added a container-local venv volume: `/clawvis/.venv`
- Updated `CHANGELOG.md` with this fix.

Validation:
- `docker compose up -d kanban-api` ‚Üí starts successfully
- `docker compose up -d` ‚Üí full stack starts
- `docker compose ps` shows:
  - `kanban-api` `Up ... (healthy)`
  - `hub`, `hub-memory-api`, `agent-service`, `telegram`, `scheduler` all running

If you want, I can also add a small cleanup step to normalize ownership of the host `.venv` so local tooling outside Docker won‚Äôt hit similar permission issues.

Docker Compose telegram service. Set TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN and TELEGRAM_CHAT_ID in .env. Without a token, stub mode logs messages instead of sending them.
‚úó SyntaxError: Unexpected non-whitespace character after JSON at position 3 (line 1 column 4)

when testing telegram, why ?
</user_query>

I‚Äôll trace the Telegram ‚ÄúSend test‚Äù request path end-to-end (Hub UI ‚Üí API route ‚Üí telegram service response) to find where non-JSON output is being parsed as JSON and why that happens in your setup.

input.command: docker compose exec -T hub sh -lc "wget -qO- --server-response http://telegram:8094/health 2>&1 | sed -n '1,12p'"
input.description: Check telegram health endpoint from hub

input.command: docker compose exec -T hub sh -lc "wget -qO- --server-response --post-data='' http://telegram:8094/test 2>&1 | sed -n '1,14p'"
input.description: Check telegram test endpoint from hub

content.text:
Because the Hub is calling Telegram endpoints that don‚Äôt exist.

- The UI button sends `POST /api/hub/telegram/test` and expects JSON.
- The Telegram service only exposes `POST /send` (no `/test`, no `/health`).
- Through nginx proxy (`/api/hub/telegram/*` -> `telegram:8094/*`), those become `/test` and `/health` on the Telegram container.
- Telegram returns `404 Not Found` as plain text, and the frontend does `await r.json()` on that text.
- Parsing `"404 Not Found"` as JSON throws: `SyntaxError: Unexpected non-whitespace character after JSON ...`.

I confirmed both endpoints return 404 from inside the running stack.

If you want, I can apply the minimal fix now:
1) add `/health` and `/test` routes in `services/telegram/core/bot.py` (recommended), or  
2) change the frontend to call `/send` and handle non-JSON error bodies safely.

 - **Root cause**: `kanban-api` bind-mounted the full repo at `/clawvis`, reusing host `.venv` files owned by `nobody:nogroup`; `uv run` failed with `Permission denied` while replacing `bin/kanban-api`, so the container exited on boot.
 - **Fix**: `docker-compose.yml` now mounts an anonymous volume at `/clawvis/.venv` for `kanban-api`, keeping a container-local writable virtualenv while preserving the host repo bind mount.
 - **Result**: `kanban-api` reaches healthy state and full `docker compose up -d` stack starts successfully.

content.name: ApplyPatch
content.input:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: /home/lgiron/lab_perso/clawvis/services/telegram/core/bot.py
@@
 async def _http_send(request: web.Request) -> web.Response:
@@
     log.info("send.ok chars=%d", len(text))
     return web.json_response({"ok": True})
 
 
+async def _http_health(_: web.Request) -> web.Response:
+    settings = get_settings()
+    return web.json_response(
+        {
+            "ok": True,
+            "stub_mode": settings.stub_mode,
+            "token_configured": not settings.stub_mode,
+            "chat_id": settings.chat_id,
+        }
+    )
+
+
+async def _http_test(_: web.Request) -> web.Response:
+    settings = get_settings()
+    if settings.stub_mode:
+        log.info("test.stub (set TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN for real delivery)")
+        return web.json_response({"ok": True, "stub": True})
+    if _tg_app is None:
+        return web.json_response({"ok": False, "error": "bot not ready"}, status=503)
+    try:
+        await _tg_app.bot.send_message(chat_id=settings.chat_id, text="Clawvis Telegram test message.")
+    except Exception:
+        log.exception("test.telegram_error chat_id=%s", settings.chat_id)
+        return web.json_response({"ok": False, "error": "internal error"}, status=500)
+    log.info("test.ok")
+    return web.json_response({"ok": True, "stub": False})
+
+
 async def _start_http_server() -> None:
     settings = get_settings()
     http_app = web.Application()
+    http_app.router.add_get("/health", _http_health)
+    http_app.router.add_post("/test", _http_test)
     http_app.router.add_post("/send", _http_send)
     runner = web.AppRunner(http_app)
*** End Patch

content.text:
Done ‚Äî `GET /health` and `POST /test` are now added in `services/telegram/core/bot.py` and wired in the HTTP router.

What changed:
- Added `GET /health` returning JSON:
  - `ok`
  - `stub_mode`
  - `token_configured`
  - `chat_id`
- Added `POST /test`:
  - returns `{ ok: true, stub: true }` in stub mode
  - sends a real Telegram test message otherwise
  - returns JSON error on failure
- Registered both routes in `_start_http_server()`.
- Added tests for both new handlers in `services/telegram/tests/test_telegram.py`.
- Updated `CHANGELOG.md`.

Verification:
- `GET /api/hub/telegram/health` now returns valid JSON (no parse error path anymore).
- `POST /api/hub/telegram/test` now returns JSON too.

Current runtime note:
- Your `/test` currently returns `{"ok": false, "error": "internal error"}` because `TELEGRAM_CHAT_ID` is effectively `0` (`chat not found` in telegram logs).  
  So the route works, but delivery fails due to config.

Set a valid `TELEGRAM_CHAT_ID` in `.env`, then restart `telegram` (`docker compose up -d telegram`) and test again.
